food_subgroup: Grilled eel with steamed rice set meal

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for unagi kabayaki set meal; closest parent 'eel food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW covers cooked eel as closest parent; no specific code exists for the full unagi set meal with rice.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for unagi set meal. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this dish.

main_ingredients: soy-based tare glaze (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard unagi set meal consisting of approximately 100–120 g of grilled eel over approximately 200–230 g of steamed white rice, totalling approximately 350 g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Unagi (eel), cooked, with rice — composite estimate based on USDA FoodData Central: Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) and Cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756), combined in a 35:65 eel-to-rice ratio by weight, adjusted for tare glaze contribution to sodium and sugar.
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the complete unagi set meal. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked eel (FDC ID 175136) at 35% weight proportion and cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756) at 65% weight proportion, then adjusting sodium upward by approximately 180 mg/100 g and sugar upward by approximately 3 g/100 g to account for the soy-mirin tare glaze. This is a medium-confidence composite estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, cooked (GI ~64–72); composite meal GI estimated at ~54 after protein and fat attenuation from eel and tare components.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for unagi set meal. White rice GI of approximately 64–72 (University of Sydney GI Database) is attenuated to approximately 54 for the composite meal due to the protein and fat content of the eel component, consistent with established mixed-meal GI attenuation methodology (Brand-Miller et al.). Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 75.25) / 100 ≈ 40.6, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 21.5 × 3.5 = 75.25 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based tare glaze
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit unagi set consumption to occasional meals, as the soy-mirin tare glaze contributes approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,470 mg per standard 350 g serving), representing over 60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (40.6) from the large white rice portion, despite the low glycemic index of the overall meal.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should practice strict portion control when consuming Unagi Set, as the large serving size contributes a high glycemic load of approximately 40.6, which can significantly impact blood glucose levels. Opting for smaller portions or pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables is recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from eel offset by high sodium from tare glaze
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unagi provides beneficial long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) and vitamin D that support cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from the tare glaze warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who must manage blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Set (うなぎセット; 鳗鱼套餐) is a Japanese set meal featuring kabayaki-style grilled freshwater eel (unagi, Anguilla japonica) served over steamed white rice, accompanied by a savoury-sweet soy-based tare glaze made from soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. The eel is traditionally butterflied, skewered, steamed to remove excess fat, then grilled over charcoal or a gas grill and repeatedly basted with tare sauce to develop a lacquered, caramelised exterior. The result is tender, moist eel with a rich umami flavour, a slightly sweet glaze, and a delicate smoky aroma. In Singapore, unagi sets are commonly served as Japanese bento-style meals at casual Japanese restaurants and food courts. Nutritionally, unagi is a notable source of high-quality protein (approximately 9.8 g per 100 g of the set), omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin D (approximately 5 mcg per 100 g), vitamin B12 (approximately 1.8 mcg per 100 g), and vitamin E. The white rice base provides the primary carbohydrate load. The tare glaze significantly elevates sodium content (approximately 420 mg per 100 g), making this dish a high-sodium meal that warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load is high due to the white rice component, and individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should practise strict portion control. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with soy or wheat allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in unagi set?
Is unagi set high in sodium?
うなぎセット カロリー

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry covers the complete unagi set meal as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked eel and cooked white rice in a 35:65 weight ratio with sodium and sugar adjustments for the tare glaze. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with mixed-meal attenuation. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175136 (eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) and FDC ID 169756 (white rice, cooked), combined at a 35:65 eel-to-rice weight ratio, with sodium adjusted upward by ~180 mg/100 g and sugar by ~3 g/100 g to account for the tare glaze. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0; this is confirmed negligible/trace per USDA source data for cooked eel and white rice — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for unagi set meal exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. Serving size of 350 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant portion; actual restaurant portions may vary. 5. Halal status is listed as false because traditional tare sauce contains mirin (rice wine) and sake (rice wine), which are not halal-certified; some restaurants may offer halal-certified versions. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302051 and FoodEx2 code A0CJW are used as closest-parent anchors; no exact ontology code exists for the complete unagi set meal. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA composite and are approximate. 8. The cuisine_tags in the input included Korean and Chinese alongside Japanese; these are likely delivery-platform categorisation artefacts and are not reflected in the canonical cuisine field, which is set to Japanese.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' to align with glycemic_index 54, as per University of Sydney GI Database definitions (GI <= 55 is low).
Updated health_context.type2_diabetes.key_factor and guidance to emphasize the high glycemic load per serving despite the low glycemic index, due to the large carbohydrate portion.
Updated llm_training.natural_language_description to reflect the high glycemic load more clearly.
